map.subtitle: Big Idea 5: Impact of Computing   •   1 day   •   LO IOC-1.C
map.intro: Everything for this topic lives in this folder. Here is how the three surfaces fit together.
folderItems: Slides — Teacher Edition, Day 1 (speaker notes on every slide: timing, share-out cues, misconception alerts)
Teacher Guide (pacing with the QOTD warm-up, misconceptions, differentiation, CED alignment, auto-derived quiz key)
Guided Notes — Day 1 (student fill-in + answer KEY)
Exercise 1: The Maple Hollow Access Audit and Exercise 2: Divide Diagnosis Clinic (student + answer KEY)
Discussion — Who Closes the Gap? (3-part: Opening, Mid-lesson, Closing — all inside the single teach day)
Quiz (student + answer KEY — 7 questions on LO IOC-1.C, canonical in the Day-1 master JSON)
map.surfacesHeading: How the three surfaces fit together
surfaces: Slides = the instruction you project and teach from (one teach day: what the divide is — its definition and dimensions — then who it affects, the issues it raises, and the three levels of action that move it).
This folder = your private scaffolding — keys, pacing, and facilitation that are deliberately NOT posted to the site.

Website = student practice that paper cannot do: instant grading, per-answer feedback, and mastery tracking. The student versions of the exercises and quiz live online; the answer keys stay with you and the site never displays them.

Scheduling: Topic 5.2 is a 1-day topic. The quiz runs as the opener of the NEXT class (spaced retrieval), so the topic never silently consumes a second day. Topic 5.3 (Computing Bias) begins immediately after the quiz.

exercise1.title: The Maple Hollow Access Audit
exercise1.intro: You are the digital-inclusion auditor hired by the (fictional) town of Maple Hollow. Five households and institutions are on your audit log. For each entry you will diagnose which characteristic of the digital divide is at work — socioeconomic, geographic, or demographic — and propose actions at the individual, organization, and government levels. Part A works the audit log; Part B extends the same skills. Everything maps to CB Learning Objective IOC-1.C.
exercise1.scenarioTitle: Audit Log: Five Stops in Maple Hollow
exercise1.narrative: The town council exported five representative cases from its connectivity survey. Your job is to audit them. Read the log row by row, then answer Part A. Use the three-step method: name the characteristic driving the gap, note who is affected (a group, an individual, or both), then match an actor and action to the barrier.

rows: Farmhouse on Orchard Lane, 6 miles out
None — no provider runs lines that far
The kids do homework in the school parking lot after hours
Homes 15 minutes away in town have three fiber options
The Riva family, downtown apartment
None — fiber reaches the building, but every plan costs too much
Four people share one prepaid smartphone
The building's wiring is ready; the monthly bill is the barrier
Silver Pines senior residence
Free building Wi-Fi, donated tablets in every room
Most tablets are still in their boxes
Residents say nobody ever showed them how to start
Maple Hollow Public Library
The town's only free public high-speed access
Every seat is taken from 3 to 7 p.m. on school nights
It closes at 7 p.m. and sits 40 minutes from the farms
A junior at the high school, phone-only
A 2 GB monthly data plan, no home connection
Runs out of data a week before each month ends
Assignments assume streaming video and cloud slideshows

exercise1.scenarioNote: Cases adapted from the Day 1 bell ringer and the diagnose-the-gap worked table; terminology and EKs follow the College Board AP Computer Science Principles Course and Exam Description (Effective Fall 2023).
exercise1.predict: Before you answer Part A, write one sentence: which one log entry would the town misdiagnose most easily, and what single detail in its row points to the real barrier?

partA.q: Entries 1 and 2 both lack home Internet — for different reasons. Name the characteristic behind each entry, and explain why the CED's definition of the digital divide covers both cases even though only one family has an affordability problem.
partA.answer: Entry 1 is a GEOGRAPHIC gap: where the farmhouse sits determines what infrastructure reaches it — no provider runs lines that far, while homes 15 minutes away have three fiber options (EK IOC-1.C.1). Entry 2 is a SOCIOECONOMIC gap: the infrastructure is present, but the monthly cost blocks the family. Both are the digital divide because EK IOC-1.C.2 defines it as differing access to computing devices and the Internet based on socioeconomic, geographic, OR demographic characteristics — any one characteristic suffices. Full credit requires both labels plus the definition's 'or,' not just 'one is location, one is money.'

partA.q: Entry 3 has free Wi-Fi AND free devices, yet the residents stay offline. Name the characteristic at work, state what this entry proves access requires beyond hardware, and use EK IOC-1.C.3 to say whether this is a group effect, an individual effect, or both.
partA.answer: The characteristic is DEMOGRAPHIC — age (EK IOC-1.C.1): infrastructure and devices are present and free, so the remaining barrier is the skills and confidence to use them ('nobody ever showed them how to start'). The entry proves access requires more than hardware — usable access includes knowing how to begin. Under EK IOC-1.C.3 this is BOTH: the residents of Silver Pines are affected as a group (a population sharing the barrier), and each resident with a boxed tablet is an affected individual. Strong answers say explicitly that buying more equipment would not move this entry.

partA.q: For Entry 1 and Entry 2, propose one action at EACH of the CED's three levels — individual, organization, government — matched to that entry's actual barrier. Then state which single action would change the most households in Maple Hollow, and defend the pick.
partA.answer: Entry 1 (geographic): individual — a neighbor in town shares downloaded materials or hosts a study evening; organization — the school lends take-home hotspots or the library runs extended rural bookmobile Wi-Fi; government — fund rural broadband buildout to Orchard Lane (the barrier is missing lines, so government-scale infrastructure is the deep fix). Entry 2 (socioeconomic): individual — a relative adds the family to a shared plan; organization — the ISP offers a verified low-cost plan; government — a connectivity subsidy for low-income households. Per EK IOC-1.C.5, the divide is affected by actions of individuals, organizations, and governments — all three count. Best single action: a defensible pick is the government buildout (it is the only lever that reaches every unserved farm at once), but any choice earns credit if matched to barrier and scale.

partA.q: Entries 4 and 5 describe students who technically 'have' Internet — a library seat or a 2 GB plan. Use all three issue words from EK IOC-1.C.4 — equity, access, and influence — to explain why your audit still flags both entries.
partA.answer: EQUITY: the same schoolwork costs these students more time, money, and planning than it costs a home-fiber classmate — identical assignments, unequal burdens. ACCESS: what they have is capped, time-boxed, and distant — the library closes at 7 and sits 40 minutes from the farms; the 2 GB plan dies a week early — so their access is thinner than the word suggests. INFLUENCE: students who can barely get online are absent from the online spaces where opinions are gathered and decisions get made, so their needs stop being visible. Per EK IOC-1.C.4 these issues arise both globally and locally — this audit is the local case.

exercise1.partBIntro: Answer in complete sentences using the CED vocabulary. These are the same items available on the site exercise.

partB.q: The town council proposes one complete fix: 'an identical tablet for every household in Maple Hollow.' Using equity vs. equality and at least two audit entries, evaluate the proposal.
partB.answer: The proposal is EQUALITY — the identical resource for everyone — not EQUITY, which matches resources to need (the fairness issue EK IOC-1.C.4 raises). It fails against the log: Entry 1 gets a tablet with no lines to connect it to (geographic barrier untouched); Entry 3 already has boxed tablets and needs training, not more hardware (demographic barrier untouched); Entry 2 is helped only partially, since the monthly service bill — not the device — is the blocker. An equitable plan would pair buildout for the farms, a subsidized plan for the Rivas, and coaching at Silver Pines. Any two entries argued correctly earn full credit.

partB.q: The mayor says: 'Our country ranks among the most connected in the world, so Maple Hollow does not have a digital divide — that is a problem for developing nations.' Use the full text of EK IOC-1.C.1 and the audit log to correct the mayor.
partB.answer: EK IOC-1.C.1 says Internet access varies between socioeconomic, geographic, and demographic characteristics AS WELL AS between countries — the between-countries clause is one part of the EK, not the whole of it. A national ranking is an average, and averages hide local variation: this audit found a geographic gap (Entry 1), a socioeconomic gap (Entries 2 and 5), and a demographic gap (Entry 3) inside one small town in a highly connected nation. Per EK IOC-1.C.4, the issues arise both globally and locally; the mayor is keeping the global half and deleting the local half.

partB.q: ENRICHMENT: Researchers sort divide cases into finer sub-types: availability (no service exists), affordability (service exists, price blocks it), skills (service and devices exist, know-how blocks use), and quality (connected, but too capped or slow for real work). Classify entries 1, 2, 3, and 5 — and explain why the CED's characteristic labels are still required on the exam.
partB.answer: Entry 1 = AVAILABILITY (no lines reach the farmhouse); Entry 2 = AFFORDABILITY (fiber reaches the building, the bill blocks it); Entry 3 = SKILLS (free Wi-Fi and devices, nobody showed residents how to start); Entry 5 = QUALITY (connected via a 2 GB cap that cannot carry streaming-and-cloud schoolwork). The sub-types sharpen the prescription — buildout vs. subsidy vs. training vs. a better plan — but the AP exam grades in the CED's terms: each case must still be tied to a socioeconomic, geographic, or demographic characteristic (EK IOC-1.C.1–C.2). The sub-type is the lens; the characteristic is the answer.

exercise2.title: Divide Diagnosis Clinic
exercise2.subtitle: 4 consults - diagnose and prescribe
exercise2.intro: You are the digital-inclusion consultant on call. Four clients arrive, each describing an access gap. For each consult, name the characteristic driving the gap using CED vocabulary (socioeconomic, geographic, or demographic — or a between-countries gap), then prescribe who could act — an individual, an organization, or a government — with a concrete action matched to the barrier. Advanced students also name the sub-type of divide for a sharper diagnosis. Vague 'get them online' advice scores zero.
exercise2.standard: CB Standard (required): characteristic named with CED vocabulary + one actor level and a concrete action, for all 4 consults. Enrichment (optional): also name the sub-type (availability, affordability, skills, or quality) and what it changes about the prescription.
exercise2.methodHeading: Three-Step Method - use for every consult
method: Anchor your gut diagnosis before analyzing. Writing your own answer first keeps a convincing wrong option from swaying you.

Match the evidence to the CED: socioeconomic, geographic, or demographic characteristics — or a between-countries gap. The reason access differs IS the diagnosis, so rule out the characteristics the evidence eliminates.

Name who could act — an individual, an organization, or a government — and the concrete action, matched to the barrier. The CED credits all three levels, so a diagnosis with no actor is an incomplete consult.
exercise2.enrichmentNote: Enrichment (sub-type reasoning): strong consults also name the finer sub-type — availability (no service exists), affordability (price blocks existing service), skills (know-how blocks use), or quality (connected but capped or slow). The sub-type picks the prescription: buildout for availability, subsidies or low-cost plans for affordability, human coaching for skills, and better plans or lent hotspots for quality. Matching actor to sub-type is what separates a real plan from a donation photo-op.
fields: Characteristic driving the gap (socioeconomic, geographic, demographic, between countries):
Who could act (individual, organization, government) + the concrete action:
Enrichment — sub-type of divide (availability, affordability, skills, quality):

scenarios.text: Pine Ridge, a town of 900 in a mountain valley, has cell coverage on one hilltop and no wired provider at all. Students park outside the gas station in the evening to use its Wi-Fi for homework, and the clinic cannot offer video appointments. Median income in Pine Ridge matches the state average. Diagnose the gap and prescribe who should act.

key.signs: no provider serves the valley at any price, and the income detail deliberately rules out a socioeconomic reading — this is a GEOGRAPHIC characteristic (EK IOC-1.C.1): where residents live determines what infrastructure reaches them. The whole town is affected as a group (EK IOC-1.C.3).
key.mechanism: the gap works through missing infrastructure, so the prescription must build some — a GOVERNMENT action fits best: state or federal rural-broadband funding to run lines into the valley (EK IOC-1.C.5). An organization-level bridge (the school lending hotspots, the gas station extending hours) helps now but does not remove the barrier. Defense against the classic error: no amount of individual budgeting fixes a wire that does not exist.
key.subtype: AVAILABILITY — the purest form: service cannot be bought at any price. Availability gaps are why 'the market will handle it' fails in low-density places; buildout economics need public investment or subsidy, which is exactly why the CED lists governments among the actors.

scenarios.text: In the city of Harborview, every block is served by three competing providers, yet the school district's survey shows a third of households rely on phone-only plans. The map of phone-only homes matches the map of lowest household incomes almost exactly. A council member argues the survey must be wrong, since 'the whole city is wired.' Diagnose the gap and answer the council member.

key.signs: service is available on every block, so geography is ruled out; the overlap between phone-only homes and lowest incomes points to a SOCIOECONOMIC characteristic (EK IOC-1.C.1–C.2) — the wires reach the buildings, the monthly price does not fit the budgets. The council member is confusing available infrastructure with actual access.
key.mechanism: the gap works through price, so prescribe actors who can move price: an ORGANIZATION (a provider offering a verified low-cost plan, the district lending hotspots) or a GOVERNMENT (a connectivity subsidy for qualifying households) — both levels the CED names (EK IOC-1.C.5). Defense: 'the whole city is wired' describes the map, not the households; EK IOC-1.C.3 lets the divide affect individual families inside a fully wired group.
key.subtype: AFFORDABILITY — with a QUALITY edge: phone-only families are not offline, they are underconnected, able to scroll but not to write essays or attend video classes. Naming the quality edge explains why 'they already have phones' is not a rebuttal.

scenarios.text: An international education nonprofit runs the same online curriculum in two partner schools. In Country A, 95% of students have home broadband and the class forum is busy every night. In Country B, 12% of homes are connected; students copy lessons by hand from one shared computer, and none of them have ever posted to the forum where the curriculum gets revised. Diagnose the gap and state what the forum detail proves.

key.signs: the two schools run identical programs, so the difference is not the curriculum — access varies BETWEEN COUNTRIES, the fourth clause of EK IOC-1.C.1. Both groups of students are affected as groups, and each hand-copying student is an affected individual (EK IOC-1.C.3).
key.mechanism: the gap works at national scale — infrastructure and cost conditions across a whole country — so the heaviest actors are GOVERNMENTS (national connectivity investment in Country B) and ORGANIZATIONS (the nonprofit routing device grants and offline-first materials to the side with 12% coverage), per EK IOC-1.C.5. Defense: the forum detail is the INFLUENCE issue from EK IOC-1.C.4 made concrete — the curriculum is revised by whoever can post, so Country B's students have no voice in a product built for them; the divide raises equity, access, and influence issues globally, not just locally.
key.subtype: AVAILABILITY at national scale, and the forum silence shows why influence is its own issue: even a perfect curriculum drifts toward the connected users' needs when only they can be heard.

scenarios.text: Westbrook Towers is a subsidized apartment complex where the city already pays for free gigabit service in every unit and a local charity donated a laptop to each household. Sign-in data shows most residents over 70, and several families who recently immigrated and are still learning English, almost never connect. The city calls the project 'done.' Audit that claim.

key.signs: free service and free devices eliminate geographic and socioeconomic explanations by design — what remains is DEMOGRAPHIC (EK IOC-1.C.1): age and language shape who can actually use the access that exists. The project is not done; it has only cleared the first two barriers.
key.mechanism: the gap works through skills and confidence, so prescribe human help, not more hardware: INDIVIDUALS (volunteer residents or students running weekly coaching hours, in residents' first languages) and ORGANIZATIONS (the charity funding multilingual training alongside its laptops), with the city's role already played (EK IOC-1.C.5 — all three levels, each matched to a different barrier). Defense: EK IOC-1.C.2 defines the divide by differing ACCESS, and a connection nobody can operate is access on paper only — sign-in data, not installation records, measures the truth.
key.subtype: SKILLS — the sub-type that survives free infrastructure. It is also the cheapest to fix per person and the most often skipped, since training produces no ribbon-cutting. A complete audit reports the divide narrowed, not closed, until usage rises.

discussion.title: Who Closes the Gap?
discussion.minutes: ~20 min total - 3 parts - Opening + Mid-lesson + Closing (all Day 1)
discussion.overview: The digital divide is easy to imagine as somewhere else — another country, another county. Then a teacher assigns a video and a slideshow, and the gap opens between two desks in the same room. Across three short windows in this lesson, your group will surface the hidden costs classmates pay for the same assignment, argue about which level — neighbor, company, or government — actually moves the gap, and then spend a fictional million dollars closing it without wasting a cent on the wrong barrier.

parts.name: Part 1: Opening Discussion
parts.when: Day 1, ~6 min, after the bell ringer
parts.intro: In your group of 3-4, discuss both questions (about 3 minutes each), then share your most interesting answer with the class. No written answers required - just discuss and listen.

items.text: Question 1. Look at your list of Jordan's extra costs. Which of them can the teacher see when grading — and which are invisible? What should a teacher assume about why an assignment arrives late or unpolished?

Question 2. Map your own access honestly: name one point where your ability to get online tonight depends on a decision somebody else made — a parent's plan, a landlord's wiring, a town's coverage. How stable is that point?
Our group's most interesting finding from Part 1 (write 1-2 sentences to share):

parts.name: Part 2: Mid-lesson Discussion
parts.when: Day 1, ~6 min, after the two-sides comparison
parts.intro: In your group, complete both tasks below.

items.label: Task A - The one-sentence rule
items.text: Equality hands everyone the same thing; equity matches the resource to the need. Test your group on three fixes for Jordan: everyone gets a hotspot; only students without home Internet get one; nobody gets one, but all assignments become downloadable at school. In one sentence your whole group agrees on, state the rule for telling an equitable fix from a merely equal one.

items.label: Task B - Bet on a level
items.text: Debate for about 3 minutes: if your town could rely on only ONE level — individuals, organizations, or governments — to narrow its divide fastest, which would your group bet on? Record the pick AND the biggest cost of relying on that level alone.

parts.name: Part 3: Closing Debrief
parts.when: Day 1, ~8 min, after Common AP Traps
parts.intro: As a group, work the prompt below, then one member shares your verdict with the class.

items.label: The million-dollar grant
items.text: Your town wins a $1,000,000 digital-inclusion grant. On the table: running fiber to unserved farms, subsidizing monthly bills for low-income families, buying a device for every student, and funding trainers at the senior center. Split the million: for each dollar amount, name the characteristic it targets (socioeconomic, geographic, demographic) and the actor level doing the work — and name one spending choice that LOOKS generous but leaves the barrier standing.
Our group's verdict (the most tempting wasted dollar + one-sentence justification):
